name: Hati Hrodvitnisson
description: Hati Hrodvitnisson, whose name translates to "He Who Hates" or "Enemy," is a monstrous wolf from Norse mythology. He is the son of Fenrir and giantess Hrodvitnir, and is destined to chase the moon across the sky, just as his brother Sköll chases the sun. Hati's relentless pursuit is a continuous threat, symbolizing the impending doom of Ragnarök, the twilight of the gods. Every successful catch of the moon by Hati causes an eclipse, filling the world with fear and dread. The constant chase represents the chaotic forces that will ultimately consume the cosmos. Hati is a powerful embodiment of cosmic destruction and the cyclical nature of time in Norse belief. He is a terrifying figure whose existence underscores the fragility of the world and the inevitability of its end. The wolf is driven by an insatiable hunger and a primal instinct to devour the celestial body, reflecting the destructive aspects of nature and the untamed forces that threaten order. His existence serves as a constant reminder of the precarious balance between light and darkness, order and chaos.
subtitle: The Moon-Chasing Wolf
appearance: Hati is depicted as a gigantic, dark wolf with piercing eyes that glow with an unnatural light. His fur is often described as being the color of night, or a deep, shadowy grey. He possesses immense strength and a fearsome presence, embodying the wild and untamed aspects of nature. His jaws are powerful enough to devour celestial bodies, and his claws are sharp enough to tear through anything. He is always depicted in motion, perpetually chasing his lunar prey across the heavens.
habitat: The skies, constantly chasing the moon
behavior: Hati is driven by a primal and insatiable hunger for the moon. He is relentless and tireless in his pursuit, never stopping until he either catches his prey or Ragnarök arrives. He is a fearsome and destructive force, embodying the chaotic elements of the cosmos. His behavior is dictated by instinct and fate, making him an unstoppable and terrifying figure.
weaknesses: Fenrir's binding (indirectly, as his fate is tied to Ragnarök)

keyMyths.title: The Chase of the Moon
keyMyths.description: Hati constantly chases the moon across the sky. When he catches it, an eclipse occurs, signifying a period of darkness and chaos.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
sources.description: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Hati and his role in the cosmos.
sources.text: A primary source for Norse mythology, providing detailed accounts of Hati and his role in the cosmos.

sources.author: Various
sources.description: A collection of Old Norse poems, including verses that mention Hati and his chase of the moon.
